does: This paper introduces AgentLure, a new benchmark for evaluating prompt injection attacks against LLM agents that operate in context-dependent environments. Unlike existing benchmarks that focus on static, straightforward attacks under fully specified instructions, AgentLure captures realistic scenarios where agent behavior depends on dynamic contextual information and adversaries craft context-aware attacks adapted to specific deployment conditions. The benchmark spans four agentic domains and eight attack vectors across diverse attack surfaces, providing a comprehensive evaluation framework that exposes fundamental limitations in current defense mechanisms when faced with sophisticated, context-adaptive attacks.
connects: This work extends prior research on prompt injection attacks and LLM security by moving beyond the simplified threat models that dominated earlier studies. Previous benchmarks and defenses assumed context-insensitive settings where attacks were straightforward and independent of operational context, overlooking how real-world agent deployments depend on dynamic information flows. The paper builds on growing awareness that tool-augmented LLM agents introduce novel attack surfaces, combining insights from adversarial ML, security testing, and agent design to articulate a more realistic threat model. It responds directly to the gap between existing evaluation frameworks and the complexity of production agent systems.
enables: This work enables development of more robust defenses by establishing a realistic evaluation standard for context-aware prompt injection resilience. By providing a comprehensive benchmark with diverse attack vectors and domains, the paper creates a foundation for designing and testing defense mechanisms that account for dynamic context in agent workflows rather than operating under simplified assumptions. Future work can leverage AgentLure to develop detection systems that identify context-dependent attacks, build input filtering or agent architectures inherently resistant to adaptive attacks, and establish best practices for secure agent delegation patterns that remain robust even when adversaries understand system context.
